summaries.headline: Most High Schools Now Teaching AI Literacy
summaries.summary: Nearly 8 in 10 educators report that high school students in their districts receive lessons on what AI is and how to use it responsibly, according to a nationally representative EdWeek Research Center survey of 499 teachers, principals, and district leaders. The instruction drops significantly in lower grades, with 73 percent of middle school students receiving AI literacy lessons, 38 percent of 4th and 5th graders, and just 8 percent of pre-K through 3rd grade students. The uneven implementation across grade levels reveals the challenge schools face in building coherent, age-appropriate AI literacy pathways at a time when global education leaders increasingly view these skills as essential for both academic success and future employment.

summaries.headline: AI Use Stalls Among Teachers Despite Industry Hype
summaries.summary: Teachers report using AI tools "a lot" at just 3% this year, barely up from 2% last year, according to an Education Week survey. Dan Meyer, vice president at Amplify and former math teacher, argues that AI chatbots cannot foster the high-trust relationships that 95% of students need for learning, while AI-generated lesson materials often lack the detail and coherence teachers require for classroom use. The gap between industry enthusiasm and classroom adoption suggests that AI's most promising applications may remain in administrative tasks rather than transforming core instruction.

summaries.headline: SEL Market Growth Outpaces Program Quality Evidence
summaries.summary: School leaders face a $4 billion SEL marketplace where many programs lack scientific backing despite strong evidence for explicit social-emotional instruction itself. Companies have rushed into the hot market without ensuring their specific offerings are research-based, creating a field where programs teach conflicting approaches—some still promote debunked emotion science or rely on ineffective strategies like universal breathing techniques. Districts need to evaluate their SEL investments using administrative data and explicit skill assessments rather than relying on vendor marketing claims.

summaries.headline: Universities Create New Roles for Campus AI Governance
summaries.summary: Universities are scrambling to establish governance frameworks as artificial intelligence tools rapidly spread across campus operations, from admissions to tutoring to research, often without clear oversight structures. Austin Community College formed a Collegewide AI Strategic Planning Committee in fall 2024 with representatives from academic leadership, IT, HR, and student affairs, while Cornell University named a vice provost for AI strategy and NC State created an executive director role in 2021. The interdisciplinary approach reflects how AI governance cuts across traditional departmental boundaries, requiring institutions to balance accountability with broad stakeholder input as they manage risks to data security and academic integrity.

summaries.headline: Chicago Board Vice President Resigns Amid Leadership Transition
summaries.summary: Olga Bautista, vice president of the Chicago school board and a close ally of Mayor Brandon Johnson, is stepping down to take a sabbatical focused on environmental justice work. Bautista was among the Johnson appointees who fired former CEO Pedro Martinez in late 2024 and was one of seven members who opposed the district's budget last fall, breaking with the majority over controversial pension funding. Her departure comes as Chicago Public Schools searches for a permanent superintendent and prepares for the city's first fully elected school board elections in November, though Johnson's allies will retain their board majority.

summaries.headline: State Takeovers Target Districts With Increasing Political Overtones
summaries.summary: Texas education officials have seized control of seven school districts since 2023, part of a national wave that includes at least 21 new takeovers in the past three years. Conservative governors and commissioners are targeting entire districts over individual school failures or perceived slow progress, with Texas threatening takeovers over anti-ICE protests and Florida's governor suggesting state control of Broward County while criticizing unions. Research shows takeovers rarely produce sustained improvements and disproportionately affect communities of color, yet the practice is expanding as officials abandon the careful academic justifications that once accompanied such interventions.

summaries.headline: Arkansas Geometry Teacher Challenges "Math Brain" Myth
summaries.summary: A 23-year high school math teacher in Arkansas argues that poor student performance in geometry stems from cultural beliefs about mathematical ability rather than cognitive limitations. The teacher's geometry team at Rogers High School exceeded growth targets by focusing on student mindset, supported by Stanford research showing that over 1,000 students improved both attitudes and test scores after taking a course designed to change beliefs about mathematical intelligence. The approach suggests that widespread cultural messaging about "math people" — reinforced by parents who describe themselves as "not good at math" — may be creating unnecessary barriers to student achievement in mathematics.

summaries.headline: North Newton's Dual Immersion Model Drives Literacy Results
summaries.summary: North Newton Elementary School has built its entire culture around a 50-50 dual immersion model where students learn in both English and Spanish daily, supported by 150 minutes of literacy instruction for K-3 students and 120 minutes for grades 4-5, plus intervention blocks. All teachers, including international educators, completed Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling (LETRS) training to address the needs of a transient student population that often arrives below grade level. The school's Multi-Tiered Systems of Support structure and collective lesson planning approach suggests that comprehensive language immersion programs require schoolwide cultural shifts and intensive professional development to serve diverse learners effectively.

summaries.headline: North Carolina Launches Statewide K-5 Math Alignment Initiative
summaries.summary: A statewide working group in North Carolina developed a comprehensive framework to address inconsistent math instruction that has weakened K-5 outcomes and limited student access to advanced coursework. The North Carolina Counts on Math initiative identified significant variation in curriculum quality, professional learning, and assessment alignment across districts through interviews with educators and analysis of instructional practices. The resulting recommendations create a coherent strategy linking standards-aligned materials, curriculum-based professional development, actionable assessments, and coordinated interventions to ensure every student receives rigorous math instruction regardless of ZIP code.

summaries.headline: Teacher Librarians Emerge as Essential AI Literacy Guides
summaries.summary: Teacher librarians are positioning themselves as crucial intermediaries between students and artificial intelligence tools, arguing their information literacy expertise directly translates to navigating AI-generated content. The commentary emphasizes that librarians' traditional skills in source evaluation, research methodology, and digital citizenship provide the foundation students need to critically assess AI outputs and understand algorithmic bias. Schools investing in robust library programs may find themselves better equipped to prepare students for a workforce where distinguishing reliable information from AI-generated content becomes a daily necessity.

summaries.headline: Federal Tech Grants Offer Districts Strategic Funding Pathways
summaries.summary: Federal grant programs provide K-12 districts with targeted funding streams for technology infrastructure, safety systems, and digital learning initiatives, though they represent just 8-13% of total school budgets. The E-Rate program anchors school network funding through five-year cycles, while competitive grants like the School Violence Prevention Program offer up to $500,000 for security technology with 25% local match requirements. Districts that align their planning cycles with these staggered application windows—from E-Rate's July-March timeline to SVPP's spring deadlines—can strategically layer funding sources to support comprehensive technology upgrades.

summaries.headline: Alaska Lawmakers Propose $630 Per-Student Funding Increase
summaries.summary: Alaska lawmakers introduced legislation Wednesday to boost per-student school funding by $630, costing an estimated $159 million, as districts across the state prepare for another round of deep budget cuts. The Anchorage School District plans to eliminate more than 300 teaching positions and close three schools this year, while students in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough staged walkouts Tuesday to protest similar cuts and closures. The proposal faces significant political headwinds in a deficit year, requiring the same bipartisan supermajority that overcame two gubernatorial vetoes to secure last year's smaller funding increase.

summaries.headline: Indiana Revives A-F School Grades After Six-Year Gap
summaries.summary: Indiana has finalized a new A-F grading system for schools that awards points for state test proficiency in reading and math, plus additional credit for attendance, diploma attainment, and alternative assessments like the Classic Learning Test favored by conservatives. The system gives more weight to proficiency than academic growth after feedback showed parents were misreading their children's actual performance levels, and aligns with federal requirements by making graduation rates and SAT scores standalone indicators worth 10% each of high school ratings. The grades will serve as Indiana's sole accountability measure in a federal flexibility request and could influence school closure decisions under Indianapolis's new shared accountability framework.

summaries.headline: NYC Chancellor Samuels Rebuilds Leadership Team Amid Departures
summaries.summary: Acting Chancellor Kamar Samuels prepares to announce his new cabinet Thursday as three top Education Department officials exit their roles, including Acting First Deputy Chancellor Isabel DiMola and Deputy Chancellor for School Leadership Danika Rux. DiMola, who served as the system's second in command, announced her retirement after 30 years in education, while Rux, who oversaw the district's reading and math curriculum overhauls under the previous administration, will transition to a senior advisor role within the department's legal office. The leadership shuffle comes as Samuels assembles his own team to implement priorities in the nation's largest school system, though Mayor Zohran Mamdani has revealed little about his broader education vision.

summaries.headline: Oregon Considers Longer School Year Amid Low Scores
summaries.summary: Oregon lawmakers are examining the state's relatively short school year as student achievement remains among the nation's lowest following the pandemic. The state requires just 162 instructional days compared to a national average of 180 days, with some districts operating even shorter calendars while test scores in reading and math continue to lag significantly behind pre-2020 levels. The timing suggests policymakers are connecting Oregon's limited classroom time to persistent academic struggles, though any changes would require districts to navigate funding and staffing challenges that originally drove the shorter schedules.

summaries.headline: Bibb District Takes Attendance Fight to Barbershops
summaries.summary: Bibb County School District Superintendent Dan Sims is launching "Meet Me Where You Are" community events in barbershops, parks, and malls to combat chronic absenteeism that spikes on Mondays and Fridays. The Georgia district sees 1,200 to 1,500 more students absent on those days compared to mid-week, prompting officials to bring enrollment services and learning resources directly into neighborhoods. The initiative reflects a growing recognition that attendance barriers often extend beyond school walls and require meeting families where they already gather.

summaries.headline: Hong Kong Schools Eye Safety Tech After Global Violence
summaries.summary: Hong Kong schools are reassessing classroom safety measures following a 200% surge in "classroom" searches and global incidents like a Pennsylvania assault, with administrators focusing on access control, visitor management, and privacy-compliant CCTV systems. The timing aligns with spring term planning and fiscal year-end budgeting, as aided and direct subsidy schools prioritize quick wins through existing grants while larger projects move through government e-tendering processes. Schools are balancing immediate security needs with longer-term facility improvements like modular layouts and sensor-equipped learning spaces, creating steady demand for integrated solutions that meet Hong Kong's data privacy requirements and support bilingual operations.

summaries.headline: California Schools Face Trans Rights, AI, Privacy Challenges
summaries.summary: California K-12 schools are navigating multiple policy pressures including the latest Supreme Court ruling on transgender rights, a nationwide artificial intelligence contest, and student data privacy violations. The state's education system confronts these emerging issues while Cal State campuses battle budget shortfalls, with CSU Dominguez Hills seeing enrollment drop 20% since 2020 and projecting an additional $8 million loss this year. Schools must simultaneously address legal compliance, technology integration, and data protection while maintaining educational quality amid declining resources and shifting federal guidance.

summaries.headline: Tutoring Expert Links Student Engagement to Post-Pandemic Recovery
summaries.summary: A tutoring company leader working with students ages 5-12 nationwide reports that pandemic disruptions to attention, stamina and confidence require rebuilding engagement before skill development can occur. The approach combines small-group instruction with AI-generated personalized reading passages matched to both interest and instructional level, moving beyond surface-level content to include rich vocabulary and higher-order thinking skills. The emphasis on representation and relevance in literacy materials suggests schools may need to reconsider how they balance engagement strategies with rigorous skill instruction for students still recovering from pandemic learning disruptions.

synthesis.topic_title: States Overhaul School Funding Formulas Amid Local Pressure
synthesis.whats_happening: State legislatures are advancing comprehensive school funding formula reforms rather than incremental budget adjustments. Local communities are escalating pressure on state lawmakers through formal political challenges, while transportation and specialized costs gain prominence in funding discussions.
synthesis.whats_driving: Post-pandemic enrollment shifts and inflation have exposed structural weaknesses in decades-old funding formulas. Communities that lost schools or face budget cuts are mobilizing politically, forcing lawmakers to address systemic funding inequities rather than defer with temporary fixes.
state_themes: - Legislative committees are fast-tracking funding recalibration bills through both chambers with bipartisan support
- Transportation funding is emerging as a distinct policy priority separate from per-pupil allocations
- Communities are organizing formal no-confidence votes against state funding systems when local schools face closure
- Reform efforts target formula mechanics rather than overall spending levels
state_snapshots: **Wyoming**: Senate and House reached agreement on school funding recalibration legislation after House committee approval (Wyoming Tribune Eagle)
**Iowa**: [REPORTED] Lawmakers are addressing transportation funding as part of broader education commitment discussions (Ida County Courier)
**Maine**: Lawmakers drafted legislation to reform the state's school funding formula (Portland Press Herald)
**Vermont**: A town with a closed school is pursuing a no-confidence vote against the state's funding system (Vermont Daily Chronicle)
district_actions: - Document transportation cost increases and facility maintenance backlogs for state funding formula testimony
- Join regional coalitions advocating for formula reforms rather than lobbying individually
- Prepare enrollment projection data that demonstrates funding volatility under current formulas
- Engage parent and community groups in state-level advocacy before legislative sessions conclude
watch_next: - Wyoming's funding recalibration bill movement to floor votes in both chambers
- Vermont communities' no-confidence vote outcomes and potential legislative response
- Maine's funding formula reform bill introduction and committee assignment

themes.theme: States are actively recalibrating and reforming their school funding formulas to address perceived inadequacies in current systems.
themes.evidence: Wyoming's legislature is advancing recalibration bills through both chambers, Maine lawmakers are writing bills to reform their funding formula, and Vermont communities are calling no-confidence votes on their funding system

themes.theme: School funding discussions are expanding beyond basic per-pupil allocations to include specialized needs like transportation costs.
themes.evidence: Iowa's education commitment explicitly includes both school funding and transportation funding as interconnected components

themes.theme: Local communities are becoming more vocal and organized in challenging state-level funding decisions through formal political processes.
themes.evidence: Vermont town seeking no-confidence vote demonstrates communities are using political mechanisms to express dissatisfaction with funding systems

themes.theme: Legislative momentum is building around systematic funding reform rather than incremental adjustments.
themes.evidence: Multiple states show coordinated legislative action with Wyoming advancing bills through both House and Senate committees, and Maine lawmakers proactively writing reform legislation

local_articles.title: Maine lawmakers write bill to reform school funding formula
local_articles.summary: Maine lawmakers write bill to reform school funding formula  The Portland Press Herald
local_articles.full_content:
Maine lawmakers will attempt to overhaul the state’s school funding formula that has long been criticized by school officials as unfair.

Legislation submitted Tuesday would adjust several aspects of the current model to address issues such as community poverty levels, special education and regional cost of living. The bill,LD 2226, will have a public hearing at 10 a.m. Monday.

The Essential Programs and Services formula, commonly known as EPS, was rolled out in 2005. There have been several efforts to reform it over the years — district leaders havedescribed it as inequitableand say some of its components have become increasingly antiquated — but thelast major attempt in 2013didn’t lead to any changes.

This time could be different. Districts around the state are beginning their budget processes for the next fiscal year and dealing with rapidly rising costs, and legislators seem eager to seriously consider an overhaul.

Two years ago, the education committee directed the nonpartisan Maine Education Policy Research Institute to study the topic. The institutedelivered its findingsover several presentations in 2025 and provided formal recommendations in early February, after whichthe committee initiated a billbased on the researchers’ suggestions.

“The core goals are to update so that we have an accurate reflection of the actual minimum costs that are present today,” Amy Johnson, co-director of MEPRI, said when she presented the recommendations last month. “And also to look at different ways to distribute state subsidy to improve how that works.”

The complex EPS formula calculates how much districts need to provide “essential” educational services based largely on pupil count, then determines how much of that cost the state will cover, derived from the local property tax valuation. Some districts receive less than 10% of their funding from the state, while others receive more than 80%.

One major proposal in the new bill is to integrate a district’s rate of economically disadvantaged students into the formula.

To account for the fact that property tax valuations mightnot accurately reflect the wealthof a community — like in coastal towns with high rates of seasonal residents — the bill proposes factoring in that rate to both the calculation side of the formula and the assessment of the district’s ability to pay. It’s based on the number of students eligible for free or reduced school meals, and researchers say it’s a good proxy for the poverty rate in the population.

“This is not about increasing more subsidy from the state, this is about changing how the subsidy is allocated out to districts, where districts that have more poverty can get more support from the state,” Johnson said.

Another major change laid out in the bill would update how regional adjustments to teacher salaries are made. Currently, those variations rely on a labor market area model that hasn’t been updated in two decades. The bill would replace that with a national cost-of-living index that is reviewed constantly.

The bill would also reform several aspects of the special education components of the formula, including eliminating a provision that superintendents say unfairly benefits wealthier districts.

The current formula starts by looking at the number of students with Individualized Education Plans (IEPs), then makes additional adjustments for those with more intense needs or who attend out-of-district programs. However, that number is then compared to the district’s total special education spending from the previous year, and if it’s higher than the calculated value, the district just receives the higher amount. LD 2226 proposes setting a cap at 80% of the value of the difference between those two numbers to reduce that inequity.

It also includes several proposed adjustments to how transportation costs, professional development and out-of-district special education tuition are accounted for in the formula.

Some provisions outlined in the bill are set to kick in during the 2027-28 school year, while others are undated, according to the draft legislation.

Hanging over the discussion of reform is the idea that any change to the formula would result in proverbial “winners” and “losers.” Researchersmodeled the impactof their proposed changes on funding for each of the state’s school districts, with details on how many districts gained or lost under each of the proposed adjustments.

School districts areanxious to see reformsto the formula. Portland school board Chair Sarah Lentz has been critical of the existing formula during early discussions on the budget for her district. Portland is expected to receive $4 million less in state funding this year.

“It’s not just Portland,” Lentz said at a board meeting last week. “Any city that has high valuation, this formula just doesn’t work as intended.”

Biddeford Superintendent Jeremy Ray is also calling for reforms and recently published an analysis of how lower-income municipalities are underserved by the current formula.

“The way schools are funded right now means districts like Biddeford are left out to dry,” Rep. Marc Malon, a Democrat who represents part of the city, said of Ray’s report. “I don’t think there’s intent behind that, but changes are needed.”

Staff Writer Sydney Richelieu contributed reporting.

Riley covers education for the Press Herald. Before moving to Portland, she spent two years in Kenai, Alaska, reporting on local government, schools and natural resources for the public radio station KDLL...More by Riley Board

local_articles.title: Town with closed school seeks no-confidence vote in state’s school funding system
local_articles.summary: Town with closed school seeks no-confidence vote in state’s school funding system  Vermont Daily Chronicle
local_articles.full_content:
The voters of the small town of Windham will consider two ballot questions expressing a vote of no confidence in the state’s education funding system, including the work-in-progress of Act 73.

Windham Elementary School has been closed since 2023, when financial, staffing and legal troubles led the Town to shutter the school and tuition children to nearby Townsend. Town Meeting voters in 2024 opted to sell the asbestos-ridden building to the Town for $1 for possible use as a community center. According tothe Commons, the community newspaper for Windham County, school voters will be asked to approve a two percent funding increase.

This year, voters in the northern Windham County town with no local school are nonetheless being asked to approve a two percent school spending climb, with an uncertain impact on the eventual tax rate, due to the current state school funding model.

‘Shall the Town of Windham declare that the current statewide funding system has failed the people of Windham and is no longer capable of providing the equitable and convenient education guaranteed under the Vermont Constitution, and that repeated legislative inaction, including the flawed implementation of Act 73, has produced unsustainable property tax burdens that disproportionately harm small rural towns?’

The second ballot question asks voters to:

“Direct the Schoolboard to formally communicate this position to the Vermont Legislature and to notify state officials that Windham is prepared to explore all available legal and political avenues to protect its taxpayers and students unless meaningful, structural reform is enacted during the current legislative session.”

Legislators for the Town of Windham are:

Passed last year, Act 73 requires lawmakers this year to adopt a school governance and funding system that reduces costs and improves outcome. At present the Legislature has at least two different models, one in the Senate, another in the House, with no reconcilation apparent at present. The Legislature will return from its Town Meeting break next Tuesday and attempt to reach an agreement before adjournment, which usually occurs in May. However, Gov. Phil Scott has said he will not allow adjournment without a suitable bill for him to sign.

local_articles.title: Wyoming Senate, House agree on school funding recalibration bill
local_articles.summary: Wyoming Senate, House agree on school funding recalibration bill  Wyoming Tribune Eagle
local_articles.full_content:
CHEYENNE — The Wyoming Legislature’s K-12 public school funding recalibration bill is headed to the governor.

On Thursday, the Senate voted 31-0 to unanimously concur with the House’s position onSenate File 81, “K-12 public school finance-2.” The bill sets forth a new funding model necessary to provide money for the state’s 48 school districts, a constitutionally mandated process the Legislature is required to undertake every five years.

However, this is the first time since 2010 that the Legislature has agreed on a recalibration bill, even though efforts to re-evaluate the model have taken place during that time. Together, the House and Senate considered 30 amendments to the bill this session.

“There were 18 proposed amendments down in the House. A lot of different things could have changed, but at the end of the day, not a lot really did change,” Sen. Chris Rothfuss, D-Laramie, who sat on the Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration, told his colleagues Thursday as he urged them to vote for concurrence on the bill.

“It’s actually in very good shape,” Rothfuss said, explaining that the House made technical corrections to the bill, as well as removed a 233% cap on superintendents’ salaries.

One amendment, he said, took out a regional cost adjustment calculation for Teton County teacher salaries, “but that was put back in in the next amendment ... so those two canceled each other,” Rothfuss said.

Rothfuss said he had expected to go to a joint conference committee with the House over the bill, but there was no reason to, as it returned to the Senate much like it left. Sen. Tim Salazar, R-Riverton, said the bill represents a bipartisan effort to fully fund Wyoming schools for the first time in 15 years.

“I compliment not only the Senate, but also those folks on the other end of the building for their efforts,” Salazar said. “This concurrence is not by accident. Your recalibration committee worked hard. I also want to thank staff for their efforts.”

Vocal opponents said early in the 68th Wyoming Legislature’s budget session that the bill would have reduced teacher numbers while increasing class sizes as it came out of the Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration. As it heads to Gov. Mark Gordon’s desk, the bill includes an additional $250 million beyond where it started this session, and class-size numbers that are closer to existing levels.

Without the recalibration bill, current law would have generated $1.7 billion in educational spending for the biennium. If signed into law, SF 81 includes an estimated $1.9 billion in funding.

Get any of our free email newsletters — news headlines, sports, arts & entertainment, state legislature, CFD news, and more.

Carrie Haderlieis a freelance journalist who covers southeast Wyoming from her home near Saratoga.

local_articles.title: Wyoming House committee approves K-12 school funding recalibration bill
local_articles.summary: Wyoming House committee approves K-12 school funding recalibration bill  Wyoming Tribune Eagle
local_articles.full_content:
CHEYENNE — After a failure to begin K-12 school finance recalibration discussions in the Wyoming House of Representatives and a dozen amendments proposed in the Senate, a House committee heard mostly support Friday for the Senate’s bill as it currently stands.

The House Education Committee voted 6-2 in favor ofSenate File 81, “K-12 public school finance-2.” According to Wyoming State Superintendent of Public Instruction Megan Degenfelder, the bill in its current form includes a net increase of $125 million each year for K-12 education, representing a “net increase for every district in the state of Wyoming.”

“It’s imperative that the Legislature make a good-faith effort to adjust the funding model, as relates to education in the state of Wyoming, this year,” she said. “This is difficult work, and has required great compromise, and there’s a reason why this has not been accomplished in several years.”

The total recalculation in SF 81 amounts to an increase of $250 million over two years of education funding, she said. Without the recalibration bill, current law would generate about $1.7 billion in educational spending for the biennium. Coming out of the Senate, SF 81 included an estimated $1.9 billion in funding.

Degenfelder also noted that the bill now achieves a compromise when it comes to health insurance, teacher salaries, student enrollment number calculations, and a move to fund counselors and nurses immediately.

Wyoming Education Association President Kim Amen told the committee that as it stands, the WEA is in support of the recalibration bill. The organization understands that districts still have concerns, but like the Department of Education, WEA appreciates the work it took to get the bill to its current point.

“This bill is very good, from where it started,” Amen said. “It is as close to constitutionally funded as we have been in a very long time.”

After thanking the committee for its work, she said she supports possible future adjustments to the bill that would address existing concerns from school superintendents and business managers.

Justin Pierantoni, superintendent of Laramie County School District 2, expressed concern over the still-siloed instructional funding included in SF 81, rather than educational block grant funding currently allocated. Curtis Rankin, Weston County School District 7 school board member, shared similar concerns, explaining that in his rural district, money allocated for specific purposes doesn’t always work as intended.

“We are a very small school district, and it’s very important for us to be able to move money around so that we can cover all of our costs,” he said, adding that while his district struggles to fund its property and capital insurance, it has plenty for school nutrition.

“We’ve got ranchers that donate meat, we’ve got people that donate vegetables,” Rankin said.

Rep. Martha Lawley, R-Worland, noted earlier in the meeting that she’d heard continued concerns about the change from a block grant to categorical funding for the districts. Sen. Chris Rothfuss, D-Laramie, who served on the Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration, said the siloed model was one area on which the committee could “find common ground” early on to ensure funding goes to teachers in classrooms.

Rep. JD Williams, R-Lusk, offered an amendment that was not adopted by the committee to remove language in the bill that siloed funding.

“There’s good reason to have it in there, but there’s also a good reason to give small districts the latitude to do what they need to do,” Williams said. “I believe this conversation is a local conversation, and my local school boards need to have flexibility to manage their own business.”

At Lawley’s suggestion, the committee also recommended removing a cap on superintendents’ salaries from the bill, a discussion the Senate had on third reading of the bill, but did not approve.

The recalibration bill will next go to the House Appropriations Committee, then head to the House floor for up to three readings. If passed there, changes by the House must be approved in the Senate or reconciled through a joint conference committee.

Correction: This story has been corrected to reflect that the Williams amendment to restore block grant funding was not adopted Friday.

Get any of our free email newsletters — news headlines, sports, arts & entertainment, state legislature, CFD news, and more.

Carrie Haderlieis a freelance journalist who covers southeast Wyoming from her home near Saratoga.
